macro_segments.title: The debate over whether ketoret must be made in a kli shares and its implications for redemption

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the debate: Rabbi Yossi bar Rabbi Chanina vs. Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi on making ketoret in a kli shares
Attribution of positions to Shmuel and Rabbi Yochanan; explanation via workers receiving ketoret as wages

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's leniency for leftover public offerings versus Rabbi Yochanan's adherence to normal rules

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's position: unblemished leftover animals can be redeemed despite normal prohibitions
The case of leftover male goats for a sin offering; applying the kal vachomer argument
Rabbi Yochanan's position: follow normal rules and wait for a blemish to redeem
Rabbi Shmuel bar Rav Yitzchak's radical view: repurposing a leftover sin offering as a burnt offering
The problem: how can a sin offering become a burnt offering? Two explanations: inherent mechanics vs. beis din stipulation

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on sanctifying property containing items fit for public offerings: the ketoret case

micro_segments.title: The mishnah case: sanctifying all property when it contains ketoret, which is fit only for public offerings

macro_segments.title: The debate over whether ketoret must be made in a kli shares and its implications for redemption (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's position allowing direct payment; the problem of redeeming something made in a kli shares

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on sanctifying property containing items fit for public offerings: the ketoret case (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's solution: give ketoret directly to workers as wages; Ben Azzai's alternative using money transfer

macro_segments.title: The debate over whether ketoret must be made in a kli shares and its implications for redemption (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Ben Azzai's objection to paying workers directly with ketoret; the mechanism of transferring sanctity to money

macro_segments.title: Sanctifying mixed property with animals fit for the altar: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's position: all sanctity becomes bedek habayit; sell animals to those needing offerings
Rabbi Yehoshua's position: animal sanctity becomes olah kedusah; only males become olah, females sold for shelamim
Rabbi Akiva's endorsement of Rabbi Eliezer as more consistent; Rabbi Pappias reports both approaches used
Resolution: explicit mention of animals follows Rabbi Eliezer; generic sanctification follows Rabbi Yehoshua

macro_segments.title: Case of wine, oil, and birds suitable for altar use but not as animal offerings

micro_segments.title: Items like wine (for nesachim) and oil (for menachot) and birds; selling them for their purposes
Textual problem: is this Rabbi Eliezer or a different position using proceeds for olah?

macro_segments.title: Teaching from Temurah on characteristics unique to kodshei bedek habayit versus kodshei mizbeach

micro_segments.title: First principle: generic sanctifications go to bedek habayit by default
Second principle: bedek habayit sanctity applies to any object; no restrictions on type
Third principle: there is me'ilah on the growth of sanctified bedek habayit items
Fourth principle: kohanim receive no benefit from kodshei bedek habayit unlike from kodshei mizbeach

macro_segments.title: Clarification of the debate through Rav Zeira's distinction between mixed property and pure flocks

micro_segments.title: Rav Zeira: if you sanctify mixed property, the debate applies; if only animals, all agree they go to mizbeach
Rabbi Ba bar Huna's alternative: debate applies whether property is mixed or pure animals
Rabbi Yochanan's position: the same debate applies in both scenarios

macro_segments.title: Redeeming kodshei bedek habayit that were unblemished and the residual status that remains

micro_segments.title: The question: when kodshei bedek habayit that are unblemished are redeemed, do they completely become profane?
Rav Chisda's ruling: difference between kodshei mizbeach remaining forbidden versus bedek habayit becoming permissible
Clarification: children and milk remain forbidden unless the animal later becomes blemished

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yossi bar Rabbi Chanina vs. Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
Rabbi Eliezer's position on mixed property
Rabbi Yehoshua's position on animal sanctity
Rav Zeira's reading of the dispute
Rabbi Ba bar Huna's reading of the dispute
